title: DMARC Forensic Report URI (ruf=) Missing
description: The DMARC record does not include a ruf= tag specifying a forensic report URI. Forensic reports contain message samples from emails that fail DMARC, which are invaluable for diagnosing spoofing attempts.
riskImpact: Without ruf=, you receive no forensic reports about individual spoofed messages targeting your domain. Active spoofing campaigns may go undetected until end users report them.
explanation: DMARC defines two reporting channels: rua= (aggregate reports, delivered daily) and ruf= (forensic reports, delivered per-failure). Forensic reports include message headers and sometimes body excerpts from emails that failed DMARC. They give you actionable intelligence about spoofing attempts and legitimate mail that is misconfigured.
fixSteps: Add ruf=mailto:dmarc-forensic@yourdomain.com to your _dmarc TXT record.
Use a dedicated inbox or a DMARC reporting service that can parse and alert on forensic reports.
Note: some receivers do not send forensic reports for privacy reasons.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT record
codeExamples.code: _dmarc.example.com. IN TXT "v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:dmarc-agg@example.com; ruf=mailto:dmarc-forensic@example.com; adkim=s; aspf=s"

title: DMARC Aggregate Report URI (rua=) Missing
description: The DMARC record does not include an rua= tag. Without aggregate reports, you have no visibility into how receivers are handling mail from your domain, and you cannot see if your legitimate mail is failing authentication.
riskImpact: Without rua= aggregate reports, you cannot detect SPF/DKIM misconfigurations in your mail stack, identify unauthorized senders using your domain, or track the effectiveness of your DMARC policy rollout.
explanation: DMARC aggregate reports (rua=) are sent daily by receivers that process mail claiming to come from your domain. They summarize how many messages passed and failed SPF and DKIM checks. Without them, you are flying blind: your policy may be blocking legitimate mail or letting through spoofed mail without you knowing.
fixSteps: Add rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@yourdomain.com to your _dmarc TXT record.
Consider using a DMARC reporting service (Postmark, Dmarcian, Valimail) to parse and visualize aggregate reports.
Review reports weekly while rolling out DMARC to catch legitimate mail failures before tightening policy.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT record
codeExamples.code:
_dmarc.example.com. IN TXT "v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@example.com"
# Start with p=none to monitor, then move to p=quarantine, then p=reject

title: MTA-STS (SMTP Strict Transport Security)
description: Async check: probes _mta-sts.<domain> for a v=STSv1 TXT record and fetches the policy file at mta-sts.<domain>/.well-known/mta-sts.txt.
evidence: DNS TXT query + HTTPS GET on the policy URL.
riskImpact: Without MTA-STS, inbound SMTP sessions can be downgraded from STARTTLS to plaintext by a network attacker, exposing all email content and credentials.
explanation: MTA-STS tells sending mail servers to require TLS and refuse to deliver mail if TLS cannot be established. Without it, opportunistic STARTTLS can be stripped by a MITM between sending and receiving mail servers.
fixSteps: Publish _mta-sts.<domain> TXT with v=STSv1; id=<timestamp>
Serve the policy at https://mta-sts.<domain>/.well-known/mta-sts.txt
Submit to the TLSRPT reporting address.

codeExamples.label: mta-sts.txt
codeExamples.code:
version: STSv1
mode: enforce
mx: *.example.com
max_age: 86400

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT
codeExamples.code: _mta-sts.example.com. IN TXT "v=STSv1; id=20240101000000"

title: TLS-RPT (SMTP TLS Reporting) Missing
description: Async check: probes _smtp._tls.<domain> for a v=TLSRPTv1 TXT record. TLS-RPT sends reports about SMTP TLS failures to the specified address.
riskImpact: Without TLS-RPT you receive no telemetry about SMTP TLS failures. STARTTLS downgrade attacks and certificate validation errors go undetected.
explanation: TLS-RPT is a companion to MTA-STS: it tells receivers where to send aggregate reports about SMTP TLS negotiation failures. Reports help you detect misconfigured certificates, policy violations, and potential STARTTLS stripping.
fixSteps: Publish _smtp._tls.<domain> TXT with v=TLSRPTv1; rua=https://...

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT
codeExamples.code: _smtp._tls.example.com. IN TXT "v=TLSRPTv1; rua=mailto:tls-reports@example.com"

title: SPF Exceeds 10 DNS Lookup Limit
description: The SPF record triggers more than 10 DNS lookups during evaluation. RFC 7208 mandates a maximum of 10 DNS lookups; exceeding this causes a permerror, causing SPF to fail for all messages.
riskImpact: When SPF returns permerror due to too many lookups, receivers may treat all mail from your domain as unauthenticated. Combined with a DMARC p=reject policy, this causes legitimate mail to be rejected.
explanation: Each include:, a:, mx:, ptr:, and exists: mechanism in SPF counts as a DNS lookup. Nested includes recursively count their own lookups against the 10-lookup limit. Exceeding this limit results in a permerror, which some receivers treat as a hard fail.
fixSteps: Replace include: for senders with static IP ranges using ip4: or ip6: mechanisms.
Flatten deeply nested SPF includes using tools like dmarcian's SPF Surveyor.
Verify the lookup count with: dig TXT example.com | grep v=spf1

codeExamples.label: Flattened SPF
codeExamples.code:
example.com. IN TXT "v=spf1 ip4:203.0.113.0/24 ip4:198.51.100.10 include:_spf.google.com -all"
# Use ip4:/ip6: instead of include: where possible

title: SPF Redirect Loop
description: The SPF record contains a redirect= modifier that creates a circular reference back to the original domain, causing infinite recursion and a permerror.
riskImpact: A redirect loop causes permerror on every SPF check, making all mail from your domain unauthenticated and subject to rejection by strict DMARC policies.
explanation: The SPF redirect= modifier replaces the current domain's SPF policy with another domain's policy. If that domain's SPF record redirects back to the original domain (directly or via a chain), SPF evaluators detect the loop and return permerror.
fixSteps: Audit the redirect chain and remove the circular reference.
Replace redirect= with include: where appropriate, or consolidate SPF records into a single authoritative record.

codeExamples.label: Detect the loop
codeExamples.code:
dig +short TXT example.com | grep v=spf1
dig +short TXT redirecttarget.com | grep v=spf1
# Trace until you find the circular reference

title: DMARC pct= Below 100
description: The DMARC record specifies pct= less than 100, meaning the declared policy (quarantine or reject) is only applied to a percentage of failing messages. The remainder are delivered as if no DMARC policy exists.
riskImpact: Spoofed messages that fail DMARC have a (100 - pct)% chance of being delivered to recipients, bypassing the protection DMARC is supposed to provide.
explanation: pct= is intended as a gradual rollout mechanism. Set it to 100 once you are confident that all legitimate mail passes SPF and DKIM. Leaving it below 100 indefinitely creates a partial protection gap.
fixSteps: Review DMARC aggregate reports (rua=) to ensure all legitimate mail sources pass authentication.
Increment pct= toward 100 as you fix any failing legitimate mail sources.
Set pct=100 once you are confident no legitimate mail is failing.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT
codeExamples.code: _dmarc.example.com. IN TXT "v=DMARC1; p=reject; pct=100; rua=mailto:dmarc@example.com; adkim=s; aspf=s"

title: DKIM Signature Selector (s=) Tag Missing
description: A DKIM-Signature header is present but does not include the required s= (selector) tag. Without a selector, receivers cannot look up the public key to verify the signature.
riskImpact: DKIM signatures without a selector cannot be verified. Receivers will treat the message as unsigned, and DMARC DKIM alignment will fail, potentially causing mail to be quarantined or rejected.
explanation: The DKIM s= tag specifies the selector used to construct the DNS lookup for the public key: <selector>._domainkey.<d=domain>. Without this tag, the DKIM-Signature is malformed and unverifiable.
fixSteps: Ensure your mail server is configured with a valid DKIM key and selector.
Verify: dig +short TXT <selector>._domainkey.example.com

codeExamples.label: DKIM DNS record
codeExamples.code: google._domainkey.example.com. IN TXT "v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=MIIBIjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAAOCAQ8A..."

codeExamples.label: Check DKIM selector
codeExamples.code: dig +short TXT selector._domainkey.example.com

title: BIMI Record Missing
description: No BIMI (Brand Indicators for Message Identification) record exists. BIMI displays your brand logo in the inbox of supported mail clients (Gmail, Apple Mail, Yahoo) when messages pass DMARC with p=quarantine or p=reject.
riskImpact: Without BIMI, your brand logo does not appear in supporting email clients, reducing brand visibility and the ability to distinguish legitimate emails from phishing.
explanation: BIMI is a standard for displaying verified brand logos in email clients. It requires a DMARC policy of p=quarantine or p=reject, a valid BIMI DNS record, and a VMC (Verified Mark Certificate) from a CA for full Gmail support.
fixSteps: Ensure DMARC p=quarantine or p=reject is set.
Publish a BIMI TXT record at default._bimi.yourdomain.com.
Host an SVG logo and optionally obtain a VMC for Gmail support.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT
codeExamples.code: default._bimi.example.com. IN TXT "v=BIMI1; l=https://example.com/logo.svg; a=https://example.com/vmc.pem"

title: MTA-STS Policy File Missing
description: An MTA-STS TXT record exists but the policy file at https://mta-sts.<domain>/.well-known/mta-sts.txt is not reachable. Without the policy file, the MTA-STS declaration has no effect.
riskImpact: Without a retrievable policy file, sending servers that check MTA-STS will not enforce TLS for mail delivery to your domain. STARTTLS downgrade attacks remain possible.
explanation: MTA-STS works in two parts: a DNS TXT record at _mta-sts.<domain> declares the policy ID, and the actual policy file is served over HTTPS at a well-known path. If the policy file is unreachable, the MTA-STS declaration is incomplete and sending servers will not enforce TLS.
fixSteps: Serve the policy at https://mta-sts.<domain>/.well-known/mta-sts.txt with mode: enforce.
Ensure the mta-sts subdomain is HTTPS-accessible with a valid certificate.
Verify: curl https://mta-sts.example.com/.well-known/mta-sts.txt

codeExamples.label: mta-sts.txt
codeExamples.code:
version: STSv1
mode: enforce
mx: mail.example.com
mx: *.example.com
max_age: 604800

title: SMTP Open Relay Detected
description: The mail server relays messages for any sender to any recipient without authentication. Open SMTP relays are immediately exploited by spammers and result in the server being listed on every major RBL within hours.
riskImpact: Your mail server becomes a spam distribution node. All IP addresses serving your domain will be blacklisted on major RBLs (Spamhaus, SpamCop, Barracuda), making legitimate mail from your domain undeliverable for weeks.
explanation: An open relay accepts and forwards email for any sender to any recipient. Before the 1990s, open relays were the norm. Today they are immediately abused by spammers and botnet operators to send millions of spam messages, causing severe IP reputation damage.
fixSteps: Require SMTP AUTH (SASL) for all outbound relay.
Restrict outbound relay to authenticated users and known internal IP ranges.
Test with: nmap -p 25 --script smtp-open-relay <host>

codeExamples.label: Postfix main.cf
codeExamples.code:
# Require auth for relay
smtpd_relay_restrictions =
  permit_mynetworks
  permit_sasl_authenticated
  defer_unauth_destination

mynetworks = 127.0.0.0/8 [::ffff:127.0.0.0]/104 [::1]/128

title: SMTP Banner Discloses Server Version
description: The SMTP 220 banner includes the mail daemon software name and version (e.g., Postfix 3.7.4, Exim 4.96). Version information aids attackers in targeting known CVEs.
riskImpact: Attackers who know the exact MTA version can target unpatched CVEs (STARTTLS injection, buffer overflows, configuration bypass) without needing to probe the system further.
explanation: The SMTP 220 banner is the first thing a connecting client sees. Most MTAs include their software name and version by default. This is unnecessary for legitimate mail delivery and provides intelligence to attackers scanning for vulnerable versions.
fixSteps: Set smtpd_banner in Postfix to a generic string.
In Exim, set smtp_banner to omit version information.

codeExamples.label: Postfix main.cf
codeExamples.code:
# Generic banner — no software name or version
smtpd_banner = $myhostname ESMTP

codeExamples.label: Exim
codeExamples.code: smtp_banner = ${primary_hostname} ESMTP

title: MTA-STS Mode Set to 'none' or 'testing'
description: The MTA-STS policy is set to mode: none or mode: testing. These modes do not enforce TLS for inbound SMTP connections, so STARTTLS downgrade attacks remain possible.
riskImpact: Attackers can downgrade inbound SMTP sessions from STARTTLS to plaintext, exposing email content and relay credentials. The MTA-STS declaration provides false assurance without enforcement.
explanation: MTA-STS mode: none and mode: testing tell sending servers to check the policy but not enforce it. Only mode: enforce causes sending servers to abort delivery if TLS cannot be established.
fixSteps: Change mode: testing or mode: none to mode: enforce.
Before switching to enforce, verify TLS is consistently working by reviewing TLS-RPT aggregate reports.
Update the id= value in the _mta-sts DNS record to force cache invalidation.

codeExamples.label: mta-sts.txt
codeExamples.code:
version: STSv1
mode: enforce
mx: mail.example.com
max_age: 604800

title: MTA-STS Policy ID Not Rotated
description: The MTA-STS id= field has not changed, meaning previously cached policies may not be invalidated when the policy changes. Sending servers cache MTA-STS policies for up to max_age seconds.
riskImpact: Stale cached policies prevent rapid policy changes from taking effect. During an incident where you need to change MX records or revoke TLS enforcement, sending servers may continue enforcing the old policy for hours.
explanation: The id= tag in the _mta-sts DNS TXT record serves as a cache-buster. When you update the policy file (e.g., change mode from testing to enforce, or update MX hostnames), you must also update id= to a new value. Sending servers compare the cached id against the current DNS id; if different, they fetch the new policy.
fixSteps: Update id= to a new value (e.g., a UTC timestamp like 20240101120000) whenever the policy file changes.
Also update id= when changing MX records to ensure sending servers pick up the new MX list.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT — updated id
codeExamples.code:
_mta-sts.example.com. IN TXT "v=STSv1; id=20240101120000"
# Increment id every time the mta-sts.txt policy changes

title: BIMI Without VMC Certificate
description: A BIMI record exists but does not include a Verified Mark Certificate (VMC). Without a VMC, Gmail and other strict clients will not display the BIMI logo.
riskImpact: Without a VMC, BIMI is ignored by major providers including Gmail; brand display uplift is not realized.
explanation: BIMI logos are only displayed by Gmail and a few other providers when accompanied by a Verified Mark Certificate (VMC) issued to the trademark holder. The VMC is a special certificate from DigiCert or Entrust that verifies trademark ownership.
fixSteps: Obtain a VMC from DigiCert or Entrust after your trademark is registered.
Host the VMC PEM at the URL referenced in the a= tag of your BIMI record.

codeExamples.label: BIMI with VMC
codeExamples.code: default._bimi.example.com. IN TXT "v=BIMI1; l=https://example.com/logo.svg; a=https://example.com/vmc.pem"

title: BIMI evidence= Without sha256 Hash
description: The BIMI record includes an evidence= URL without a sha256 hash fragment. Without the hash, receivers cannot verify the integrity of the referenced evidence file.
riskImpact: An attacker who hijacks the evidence URL can swap the document and impersonate your brand in recipient inboxes without the receiver detecting the tampering.
explanation: BIMI evidence URLs may include a sha256 fragment to allow receivers to verify the integrity of the evidence file. Without the hash, the evidence URL is trusted solely based on HTTPS, providing no protection against content substitution at the hosting origin.
fixSteps: Append the sha256 fragment to your evidence URL (e.g., logo.svg#sha256=<base64>).
Compute the hash: openssl dgst -sha256 -binary logo.svg | base64

codeExamples.label: Compute SVG hash
codeExamples.code: openssl dgst -sha256 -binary logo.svg | base64 -w 0

title: MX Hostname Is a CNAME (RFC Violation)
description: An MX record points to a hostname that is a CNAME alias rather than an A/AAAA record. RFC 5321 explicitly prohibits MX targets from being CNAME records.
riskImpact: Mail servers that strictly follow RFC 5321 will reject or skip this MX record, causing mail delivery failures for a subset of senders.
explanation: RFC 5321 §5.1 requires that MX target hostnames resolve to A or AAAA records directly, not via CNAME. Many mail servers follow the RFC strictly and will not follow CNAMEs for MX targets, treating them as invalid.
fixSteps: Point the MX record directly at a hostname with an A/AAAA record.
If the MX hostname is managed by a provider, use the canonical hostname they provide, not a CNAME alias.

codeExamples.label: Correct MX — no CNAME
codeExamples.code:
# Correct: MX points to a hostname with A record
example.com. IN MX 10 mail.example.com.
mail.example.com. IN A 198.51.100.10

# Incorrect: MX points to a CNAME
# example.com. IN MX 10 alias.example.com.
# alias.example.com. IN CNAME mail.example.com.  <- RFC violation

title: MX Hostname Lacks IPv6 (AAAA) Record
description: The MX hostname only resolves via IPv4 A records with no AAAA record. IPv6-only sending servers cannot connect to deliver mail.
riskImpact: IPv6-only sending environments (increasingly common in large providers like Google and Microsoft) may fail to deliver mail to your domain if your MX only has IPv4 connectivity.
explanation: Dual-stack MX hostnames are important as IPv6 adoption grows. Large mail providers like Google and Microsoft send from IPv6 addresses. If your MX server has no AAAA record, these providers must fall back to IPv4, or in strict IPv6-only environments, mail may be delayed or bounce.
fixSteps: Add AAAA records for every MX hostname.
Verify: dig +short A mail.example.com && dig +short AAAA mail.example.com

codeExamples.label: Dual-stack MX
codeExamples.code:
mail.example.com. IN A    198.51.100.10
mail.example.com. IN AAAA 2001:db8::10

title: SPF include: Without Provider _spf Prefix
description: The SPF record includes a domain that does not follow the standard _spf.* subdomain convention used by legitimate ESPs. Non-standard includes may indicate misconfiguration or an unauthorized ESP.
riskImpact: Mis-prefixed or unauthorized SPF includes may grant a third party the ability to send email on behalf of your domain, or may cause SPF to break silently if the included domain's SPF is misconfigured.
explanation: Legitimate ESPs (Google Workspace, SendGrid, Mailgun, Mailchimp) publish their allowed senders under predictable _spf.* subdomains. Includes that point to domains without this convention may be from unofficial or unauthorized mail sources.
fixSteps: Verify each include: points to a recognized provider's documented SPF subdomain.
Replace ad-hoc includes with the provider's officially documented _spf subdomain.
Audit all sending services authorized to send from your domain.

codeExamples.label: Correct include prefixes
codeExamples.code: example.com. IN TXT "v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com include:sendgrid.net include:_spf.mailchimp.com -all"

title: PLAIN or LOGIN SMTP AUTH Advertised
description: The SMTP server advertises AUTH PLAIN or AUTH LOGIN mechanisms, which transmit credentials base64-encoded but unencrypted unless STARTTLS is enforced before AUTH.
riskImpact: If STARTTLS is downgraded or not enforced, AUTH PLAIN/LOGIN transmit credentials in cleartext over the wire. An on-path attacker captures the base64-encoded credentials and trivially decodes them.
explanation: AUTH PLAIN and AUTH LOGIN encode credentials in base64 (not encrypted, just encoded). They are only safe when the SMTP session is first encrypted with STARTTLS. If STARTTLS can be stripped, credentials are exposed.
fixSteps: Enforce STARTTLS before advertising PLAIN/LOGIN auth mechanisms.
Disable PLAIN/LOGIN on non-TLS connections in your MTA config.
Pair with MTA-STS mode: enforce to prevent STARTTLS downgrade.

codeExamples.label: Postfix — enforce TLS before auth
codeExamples.code:
# Only advertise auth after STARTTLS
smtpd_tls_security_level = encrypt
smtpd_sasl_auth_enable = yes
smtpd_sasl_tls_security_options = noanonymous

title: SMTP STARTTLS Not Advertised
description: The SMTP server does not advertise STARTTLS capability. Without STARTTLS, all SMTP sessions to this server are plaintext, exposing email content and AUTH credentials in transit.
riskImpact: All inbound and outbound mail traffic to this MX server is transmitted in cleartext. Email content, headers, sender/recipient addresses, and AUTH credentials are visible to any on-path observer.
explanation: STARTTLS upgrades a plain SMTP connection to TLS. When STARTTLS is not advertised, sending mail servers cannot encrypt the session. Mail is delivered entirely in cleartext. This is particularly serious for mail that may contain sensitive personal data.
fixSteps: Enable STARTTLS in Postfix (smtpd_tls_security_level = may or encrypt).
Install a valid TLS certificate on the mail server.
Pair with MTA-STS mode: enforce so sending servers require TLS.

codeExamples.label: Postfix main.cf
codeExamples.code:
# Enable STARTTLS
smtpd_tls_security_level = may
smtpd_tls_cert_file = /etc/ssl/certs/mail.example.com.pem
smtpd_tls_key_file = /etc/ssl/private/mail.example.com.key
smtpd_tls_protocols = !SSLv2, !SSLv3, !TLSv1, !TLSv1.1
smtpd_tls_mandatory_protocols = !SSLv2, !SSLv3, !TLSv1, !TLSv1.1

title: SPF Record (Sender Policy Framework)
description: No SPF record was found at the domain apex. SPF is one of three email authentication mechanisms (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) that prevent unauthorized senders from sending mail as your domain.
riskImpact: Without SPF, any server on the Internet can send email appearing to come from your domain. Phishing, business email compromise (BEC), and spam campaigns regularly exploit domains without SPF.
explanation: SPF (RFC 7208) publishes an allowlist of servers authorized to send email from your domain as a TXT record at the apex. When a receiving server gets mail claiming to be from your domain, it checks whether the sending server's IP is in your SPF record. Without SPF, this check cannot be performed.
fixSteps: Publish a TXT record at the apex: v=spf1 <mechanisms> -all
Include all services that send mail on your behalf (Google, SendGrid, etc.).
Stay under the 10 DNS lookup limit.
Use -all (hard fail) once all legitimate senders are included.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT
codeExamples.code: example.com. IN TXT "v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com include:sendgrid.net ip4:203.0.113.10 -all"

title: DMARC Record Missing
description: No DMARC record exists at _dmarc.<domain>. Without DMARC, there is no policy telling receivers what to do with mail that fails SPF or DKIM authentication, and you receive no reports about authentication failures.
riskImpact: Without DMARC, spoofed mail that fails SPF or DKIM is still delivered to recipients. You have no visibility into who is sending email as your domain, making phishing and BEC attacks undetectable.
explanation: DMARC (RFC 7489) ties together SPF and DKIM with a declared policy (none/quarantine/reject) and reporting addresses. It is the standard mechanism for telling receivers to quarantine or reject mail that fails authentication and to send you reports about what they see.
fixSteps: Publish a TXT record at _dmarc.<domain> starting with p=none to collect reports.
Review aggregate reports (rua=) to identify legitimate mail sources.
Progress to p=quarantine then p=reject as you fix authentication issues.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT — start with p=none
codeExamples.code: _dmarc.example.com. IN TXT "v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc@example.com; adkim=r; aspf=r"

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT — enforce with p=reject
codeExamples.code: _dmarc.example.com. IN TXT "v=DMARC1; p=reject; pct=100; rua=mailto:dmarc@example.com; ruf=mailto:dmarc-forensic@example.com; adkim=s; aspf=s"

title: DKIM Record Missing
description: No DKIM public key record was found for this domain. DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) cryptographically signs outgoing messages, allowing receivers to verify the signature has not been tampered with.
riskImpact: Without DKIM, your email messages have no cryptographic signature. DMARC alignment relies on either SPF or DKIM passing; without DKIM, SPF-only alignment is more easily broken by mail forwarding, weakening your overall protection.
explanation: DKIM (RFC 6376) adds a digital signature to outgoing email headers. The receiving server looks up the public key in DNS at <selector>._domainkey.<domain> and verifies the signature. A valid DKIM signature proves the message was sent by an authorized server and was not modified in transit.
fixSteps: Generate a DKIM key pair in your mail server or ESP.
Publish the public key as a TXT record at <selector>._domainkey.<domain>.
Verify: dig +short TXT selector._domainkey.example.com

codeExamples.label: DKIM DNS TXT record
codeExamples.code: google._domainkey.example.com. IN TXT "v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=MIIBIjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAAOCAQ8AMIIBCgKCAQEA..."

codeExamples.label: Postfix + OpenDKIM
codeExamples.code:
# Generate 2048-bit RSA DKIM key
opendkim-genkey -b 2048 -d example.com -s mail -t
# Publishes: mail._domainkey.example.com

title: DNSSEC Not Enabled
description: DNSSEC is not enabled for this domain's DNS zone. Without DNSSEC, DNS responses can be forged by cache poisoning attacks, redirecting email to attacker-controlled servers.
riskImpact: Without DNSSEC, attackers can poison DNS caches to forge MX records, redirecting inbound email to attacker-controlled mail servers. SPF and DMARC DNS lookups can also be spoofed, undermining email authentication.
explanation: DNSSEC (RFC 4034/4035) uses public-key cryptography to sign DNS records. When DNSSEC is enabled and a DS record is published at the parent TLD, DNSSEC-validating resolvers can verify that DNS responses have not been tampered with. This is particularly important for email since MX, SPF, DKIM, and DMARC all rely on DNS lookups.
fixSteps: Enable DNSSEC in your DNS provider's control panel or configure it in BIND/Knot/PowerDNS.
Submit the DS record to your domain registrar.
Verify: dig +dnssec A example.com (look for 'ad' flag and RRSIG records)

codeExamples.label: Verify DNSSEC status
codeExamples.code: dig +dnssec A example.com | grep -E '(RRSIG|flags.*ad)'

title: TLS-RPT Record Missing
description: No TLS-RPT record exists at _smtp._tls.<domain>. TLS-RPT (RFC 8460) is a companion to MTA-STS that receives aggregate reports about SMTP TLS negotiation failures.
riskImpact: Without TLS-RPT, you have no visibility into SMTP TLS failures. Certificate expiry, policy misconfiguration, and potential STARTTLS downgrade attacks go undetected until mail delivery fails.
explanation: TLS-RPT collects daily aggregate reports from mail senders about TLS connection failures when delivering to your mail servers. Reports include MX hostnames, failure reasons, and counts. They are essential for monitoring the health of MTA-STS enforcement.
fixSteps: Publish a TXT record at _smtp._tls.<domain> with v=TLSRPTv1; rua=.
Use an email address or HTTPS endpoint that can receive and process JSON reports.

codeExamples.label: DNS TXT
codeExamples.code: _smtp._tls.example.com. IN TXT "v=TLSRPTv1; rua=mailto:tls-reports@example.com"

title: SPF Uses Deprecated ptr: Mechanism
description: The SPF record uses the ptr: mechanism. RFC 7208 explicitly deprecates ptr: because it triggers expensive and unreliable reverse DNS lookups that slow SPF evaluation and may cause timeouts.
riskImpact: ptr: lookups can timeout or fail intermittently, causing SPF to return temperror. Some receivers treat temperror as a pass (accepting mail), others may defer or fail delivery.
explanation: The SPF ptr: mechanism performs a reverse DNS lookup on the sending IP to find a hostname, then forward-confirms it. This is slow, expensive (counts as multiple DNS lookups), and unreliable since not all IPs have PTR records. RFC 7208 §5.5 explicitly says: 'This mechanism SHOULD NOT be published.'
fixSteps: Replace ptr: with explicit ip4:, ip6:, a:, or include: mechanisms.
Audit all senders and add their IPs or SPF include references directly.

codeExamples.label: Replace ptr: with ip4:
codeExamples.code:
# Avoid:
# v=spf1 ptr:example.com -all

# Use instead:
example.com. IN TXT "v=spf1 ip4:203.0.113.0/24 include:_spf.google.com -all"

title: ARC (Authenticated Received Chain) Not Configured
description: No ARC signing configuration is detected. ARC (RFC 8617) preserves authentication results across email forwarding hops, preventing legitimate forwarded mail from failing DMARC at the final destination.
riskImpact: Without ARC, forwarded messages (mailing lists, corporate forwarders) frequently fail DMARC at the final destination because SPF fails for the forwarder's IP and DKIM signatures are broken by list rewriting. This causes legitimate mail to be quarantined or rejected.
explanation: ARC (Authenticated Received Chain) adds a chain of signed authentication results to forwarded messages. Each intermediate handler (mailing list, forwarder) adds an ARC seal attesting to the authentication state it observed. The final receiver can trust the chain to make better delivery decisions even if the original SPF/DKIM checks fail.
fixSteps: Configure ARC signing in your MTA (Postfix with OpenARC, Exim with ARC support).
If you operate a mailing list or mail forwarder, ARC signing is especially important.
For receiving: configure your spam filter to honor ARC seals from trusted sealers.

codeExamples.label: Check for ARC headers
codeExamples.code:
# ARC headers appear in forwarded message source:
# ARC-Authentication-Results: i=1; ...
# ARC-Message-Signature: i=1; ...
# ARC-Seal: i=1; ...
